In this symbolic vision, the temple is opened to view, even to the most sacred place. The prophet, standing outside the temple, sees God’s Presence seated on the mercy-seat, raised above the ark of the covenant, between the cherubim and seraphim, and God's glory fills the entire temple. See God upon his throne. This vision explains that Isaiah now saw Christ's glory and spoke about Him, which is full proof that our Savior is God. In Christ Jesus, God is seated on a throne of grace; and through him, the way into the holiest is laid open. See God’s temple, his church on earth, filled with his glory. His train, the edges of his robes, fills the temple, the whole world, for it is all God's temple. Yet he dwells in every humble heart. See the blessed attendants by whom his government is served. Above the throne stood the holy angels, called seraphim, which means "burners;" they burn with love to God and zeal for his glory against sin. The seraphim showing their faces veiled declares that they are ready to yield obedience to all God’s commands, though they do not understand the secret reasons of his plans, government, or promises. All vanity, ambition, ignorance, and pride would be removed by one view of Christ in his glory. This awesome vision of Divine Majesty overwhelmed the prophet with a sense of his own worthlessness. We are ruined if there is not a Mediator between us and this holy God. A glimpse of heavenly glory is enough to convince us that all our righteousnesses are as filthy rags. Nor is there a man who would dare to speak to the Lord, if he saw the justice, holiness, and majesty of God, without understanding his glorious mercy and grace in Jesus Christ. The live coal may denote the assurance given to the prophet of pardon and acceptance in his work through Christ’s atonement. Nothing is powerful to cleanse and comfort the soul but what comes from Christ's satisfaction and intercession. Taking away sin is necessary for us to speak with confidence and comfort, either to God in prayer or from God in preaching; and those shall have their sins taken away who complain of them as a burden and see themselves in danger of being ruined by them. It is great comfort to those whom God sends that they go for God and may therefore speak in his name, assured that he will support them.

God sends Isaiah to foretell the ruin of his people. Many hear the sound of God’s word but do not feel its power. God sometimes, in righteous judgment, gives men up to blindness of mind because they will not receive the truth with love. But no humble seeker after Christ need to fear this awful doom, which is a spiritual judgment on those who still hold fast their sins. Let every one pray for the enlightening of the Holy Spirit that he may perceive how precious are the Divine mercies by which alone we are secured against this dreadful danger. Yet the Lord would preserve a remnant, like the tenth, holy to him. And blessed be God, he still preserves his church; however professors or visible churches may be cut off as unfruitful, the holy seed will shoot forth from whom all the numerous branches of righteousness shall arise.
